entries.management_implication: Use as active statewide elk policy baseline.
entries.model_use_guidance: Policy context only; not direct p_draw input.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Approved on December 1, 2022 and in effect for 10 years until December 2032.

entries.management_implication: Expect structured policy review cycle.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Two mid-plan reviews spaced 3-4 years apart, scheduled in spring 2026.

entries.management_implication: State-level abundance anchor.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Current statewide population estimated at approximately 84,390 animals.

entries.metric: Statewide population objective (sum of unit objectives)
entries.management_implication: Herd units roll up into statewide objective.
entries.model_use_guidance: Management context; not direct draw mechanic.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Current population objective for elk statewide is 78,990.

entries.management_implication: High historical growth phase from low baseline.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Population grew from about 18,000 to 58,000 (average annual growth rate 1.08).

entries.management_implication: High-opportunity general-season lane is foundational.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Issued over 47,000 general season permits in 2022.

entries.value: 17500 adult any bull; over 2200 youth any bull; 15000 spike; over 12700 archery
entries.management_implication: Permit-mix design drives access pattern.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Permit categories listed for adult any bull, youth any bull, spike, and archery.

entries.metric: General season hunter success (all general season elk hunts)
entries.management_implication: Baseline opportunity yield is modest.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Success rates in 2021 averaged 15.5% across general season elk hunts.

entries.metric: General season harvest and hunters afield
entries.management_implication: Denominator context for success rates.
entries.evidence_excerpt: 5,755 elk harvested by 37,211 hunters afield.

entries.management_implication: Archery is lowest-success general lane in cited baseline.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Archery hunters recorded the lowest success rates (11.4%).

entries.management_implication: Multi-season improves access yield for spike hunters.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Multi-season spike only success was 22.9%.

entries.management_implication: Highest success in cited general-season categories.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Multi-season any bull success was 28.2%.

entries.metric: Traditional LE units managed by average age of harvested bulls
entries.management_implication: Age objectives are central to LE permit decisions.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Traditional limited entry units are managed for average age of harvested bulls.

entries.metric: Average antler growth achieved by age 6.5
entries.management_implication: Supports lower age objective tradeoff rationale.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Most bulls achieved 96% of antler growth potential by 6.5 years old.

entries.metric: Main beam length achieved by age 6.5
entries.management_implication: Supports age-structure policy decisions.
entries.evidence_excerpt: 97% of main beam length achieved by 6.5 years old.

entries.metric: Main beam length increase after age 8.5
entries.management_implication: Very old age classes offer limited additional antler gain.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Length does not increase after an elk reaches 8.5 years old.

entries.metric: Estimated opportunity gain from lowering age objectives by one year
entries.management_implication: Explicit policy tradeoff between age target and opportunity.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Lowering age objectives by one year can increase opportunity by 40-50%.

entries.metric: Adult female annual survival including harvest
entries.effective_period: Wasatch and surrounding units study period
entries.management_implication: Harvest pressure materially affects adult female survival.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Annual survival ranged from 75 to 81 percent when harvest was included.

entries.metric: Adult female annual survival excluding human harvest
entries.effective_period: Wasatch and surrounding units study period
entries.management_implication: Baseline survival is high absent hunting mortality.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Survival ranged from 93 to 98 percent in absence of harvest by humans.

entries.metric: Share of collared adult females killed by hunters
entries.effective_period: Wasatch and surrounding units study period
entries.management_implication: Hunter harvest is dominant mortality driver in cited study.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Over 21 percent of collared elk were killed by hunters.

entries.management_implication: Recruitment vulnerability is substantial.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Neonate survival averaged between 41 and 47 percent.

entries.metric: Collared calf first-year mortality attributed to cougars
entries.management_implication: Predation pressure is a major calf constraint in cited unit.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Up to 45 percent of collared calves were killed by cougars.

entries.management_implication: Supports productivity expectations in good-condition cohorts.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Average adult pregnancy rates have been 85%.

entries.management_implication: Large inter-unit/inter-year variability.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Pregnancy ranged from 55% to 100%.

entries.management_implication: Program used to reduce public-to-private seasonal exodus.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Division began allowing purchase of private-lands-only antlerless elk permit in 2016.

entries.management_implication: Demand pressure remains high and rising.
entries.evidence_excerpt: In 2022, 75,925 hunters applied for 3,117 limited entry permits.

entries.management_implication: Nonresident demand pressure is especially steep.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Resulting in 1:19.8 draw odds for residents and 1:70.5 for nonresidents.

entries.metric: General season any bull permit sellout acceleration
entries.value: 2018 sold out in 34 days; 2020 in less than 8 hours; 2022 in less than 6 hours
entries.management_implication: Explosive demand growth for general-season opportunity.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Any bull permits sold out increasingly quickly from 2018 to 2022.

entries.management_implication: Spike demand also highly elevated.
entries.evidence_excerpt: General season spike bull tags sold out in just nine hours in 2022.

entries.value: 15000 spike permits; up to 4500 multi-season sub-quota; up to 15000 early any-bull permits; unlimited late any-bull; unlimited general archery
entries.management_implication: Defines access-centric structure for general units.
entries.evidence_excerpt: General opportunity strategy lists fixed and unlimited permit lanes.

entries.value: unlimited general season youth bull permits on spike and any-bull units; separate limited draw-only youth any-bull hunt
entries.management_implication: Youth participation is preserved with broad access.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Unlimited youth bull permits plus limited-quota draw-only youth any-bull hunt.

entries.management_implication: Three-tier age framework controls LE quality bands.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Maintain 3 categories of age class harvest objectives.

entries.metric: Condition for increasing LE permits while maintaining quality
entries.value: any-legal-weapon 3-year average success over 75% and unit meeting/exceeding age objective
entries.management_implication: Explicit trigger for opportunity expansion.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Increase permits when 3-year success exceeds 75% and age objective is met.

entries.management_implication: Core allocation template for standard LE units.
entries.evidence_excerpt: 25% archery, 15% muzzleloader, 60% any legal weapon.

entries.metric: Any-weapon split when all three seasons exist
entries.management_implication: Intra-any-weapon distribution template.
entries.evidence_excerpt: 10% early, 30% mid, 17% late any-weapon and 3% multi-season.

entries.unit: September archery versus HAMS/restricted weapon percent
entries.management_implication: Primitive opportunities balanced across two season types.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Allocate permits 50% September archery and 50% HAMS/restricted weapon.

entries.value: 1% of combined LE permits or minimum 5 permits
entries.management_implication: Controlled extra-opportunity mechanism on qualifying units.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Permits should equal 1% of combined LE permits or a minimum of 5.

entries.value: bull:cow ratio greater than 40 bulls per 100 cows
entries.management_implication: High-ratio units can absorb additional restricted opportunity.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Recommend additional restricted/HAMS hunts on units with bull:cow ratio > 40/100.

entries.management_implication: Habitat work benchmark for carrying capacity support.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Increase forage production by annually treating a minimum of 40,000 acres.

entries.metric: Voluntary mitigation ratio for disturbance in crucial habitat
entries.unit: acres improved or conserved per acre disturbed
entries.management_implication: Large-scale development mitigation expectation.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Voluntary mitigation recommended at a 4:1 ratio.

entries.value: Use age metrics for quality context only; do not directly override permit quotas or p_draw
entries.management_implication: Preserves separation between quality indicators and draw mechanics.
entries.model_use_guidance: Non-causal draw input unless explicitly validated.
entries.evidence_excerpt: Implementation guardrail consistent with plan-governance usage.
